revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Ashinazuchi und Tenazuchi]][[en:Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi]][[es:Ashinazuchi y Tenazuchi]][[it:Ashinazuchi e Tenazuchi]][[ja:アシナヅチ・テナヅチ]][[simple:Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi]][[sr:Ашиназучи и Теназучи]][[zh:足名椎與手名椎]]


{{Short description|Japanese mythical couple|qq=}}
{{Infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| image = Tsuno-jinja Ashinazuchi & Tenazuchi-jinja.JPG
| caption = Shrine dedicated to the two of them located on the premises of [[Tsuno Shrine]]{{verification needed|date=November 2023|qq=}}
| father = [[Ōyamatsumi]]
| children = [[Kushinadahime]]
| attributes = {{ill|Ashinazuchi|qid=Q109554668|1=Ashinazuchi}}; {{ill|Tenazuchi|qid=Q109554669|1=Tenazuchi}}
}}
'''Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi''' are a pair of Japanese deities.<ref>{{cite book | last=Frédéric | first=L. | title=Japan Encyclopedia | publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press | series=Harvard University Press reference library | year=2005 | isbn=978-0-674-01753-5 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC | access-date=2020-11-21 |qq=}}</ref> They are the parents of [[Kushinadahime]], the wife of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=My Shinto: Personal Descriptions of Japanese Religion and Culture |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/~n-havens/myshinto/htmlfile/mmori02.html |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp|qq=}}</ref> The serpent killed their other 7 daughters.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Yamata no Orochi |url=http://kikuko-nagoya.com/html/yamatanoorochi.html |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=kikuko-nagoya.com|qq=}}</ref><ref name="Borgen-1990">{{Cite journal |last1=Borgen |first1=Robert |last2=Ury |first2=Marian |date=1990 |title=Readable Japanese Mythology: Selections from Nihon shoki and Kojiki |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/489230 |journal=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |volume=24 |issue=1 |pages=80 |doi=10.2307/489230 |jstor=489230 |issn=0885-9884|qq=}}</ref>
Their names mean foot stroking elder and hand stroking elder respectively.<ref name="Drott-2016">{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&dq=Ashinazuchi+and+Tenazuchi&pg=PA16 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=8 |language=en|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Herbert |first=Jean |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&dq=Ashinazuchi+and+Tenazuchi&pg=PT397 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |date=2010-10-18 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |language=en|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Borgen |first1=Robert |last2=Ury |first2=Marian |date=1990 |title=Readable Japanese Mythology: Selections from Nihon shoki and Kojiki |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/489230 |journal=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |volume=24 |issue=1 |pages=64 |doi=10.2307/489230 |jstor=489230 |issn=0885-9884|qq=}}</ref> They are considered [[Kunitsukami]].<ref name="Drott-2016" /><ref name="Borgen-1990" /> They are the only two deities of the [[Kojiki]] explicitly stated as elders.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Drott |first=Edward R. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hB7HDwAAQBAJ&dq=Ashinazuchi+and+Tenazuchi&pg=PA16 |title=Buddhism and the Transformation of Old Age in Medieval Japan |date=2016-04-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5150-7 |pages=16 |language=en|qq=}}</ref>
Ashinazuchi brought alcohol to [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] in order to kill {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|en|Yamata no Orochi|qid=Q1054437|lt=Yamata no Orochi|1=Yamata no Orochi}}<ref>{{Cite web |title='The Dragon' from Japanese mythology KOJIKI |url=https://kojiki.co/en/mythology/episode13.html |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=kojiki.co|qq=}}</ref><ref name="Borgen-1990" /> alongside Tensazuchi.<ref name="Borgen-1990" /> Susanoo got the serpent drink with the alcohol and killed it for them.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Amulets – Rokusho Jinja (shrine)website |url=https://rokusho.jp/en/index.php/amulets/ |access-date=2023-10-16 |language=ja|qq=}}</ref>
In the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the god [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], after his banishment from the heavenly realm [[Takamagahara]], came down to earth, to the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=land of Izumo|1=Izumo Province}}, where he encountered an elderly couple named Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi, both children of the mountain god [[Ōyamatsumi]]. They told him of a monstrous creature from the nearby land of {{ill|Koshi Province (Japan)|en|Koshi Province (Japan)|qid=Q1351611|lt=Koshi|1=Koshi Province (Japan)}} known as the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|en|Yamata no Orochi|qid=Q1054437|lt=Yamata no Orochi|1=Yamata no Orochi}} ("eight-forked serpent") that had devoured seven of their eight daughters. Upon hearing this, Susanoo agreed to kill the serpent on condition that they give him their sole surviving daughter, [[Kushinadahime]], to be his wife.<ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj025.htm Section XVIII.—The Eight-Forked Serpent.]</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |publisher=Princeton University Press |year=2015 |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=89–90|qq=}}</ref> After he was successful they became grandparents of [[Yashimajinumi]].<ref name="Kami">{{cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Yashimajinumi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=181 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp|qq=}}</ref> They were granted the title of Inada palace master.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Borgen |first1=Robert |last2=Ury |first2=Marian |date=1990 |title=Readable Japanese Mythology: Selections from Nihon shoki and Kojiki |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/489230 |journal=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |volume=24 |issue=1 |pages=61–97 |doi=10.2307/489230 |jstor=489230 |issn=0885-9884|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Borgen |first1=Robert |last2=Ury |first2=Marian |date=1990 |title=Readable Japanese Mythology: Selections from Nihon shoki and Kojiki |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/489230 |journal=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |volume=24 |issue=1 |pages=81 |doi=10.2307/489230 |jstor=489230 |issn=0885-9884|qq=}}</ref>
They are enshrined in [[Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine, Saitama]] representing love as a married couple alongside [[Kushinadahime]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-02-27 |title=What Is a Temple: Three Shrines of Love in Japan! - Sakuraco |url=https://sakura.co/blog/what-is-a-temple-three-shrines-of-love-in-japan/,%20https://sakura.co/blog/what-is-a-temple-three-shrines-of-love-in-japan/ |access-date=2023-10-16 |language=en-US|qq=}}</ref>
==Family tree==
{{Eight generations of Izumo|qq=}}
